No credible source contradicts the core claim.

result.evidence: Verbatim quote CONFIRMED in the actual source: "From SLAM to Situational Awareness: Challenges and Survey," Sensors (Basel/MDPI), peer-reviewed, published 2023-05-17, PMC10222985. Exact text: "Up to now, the robotics community has focused chiefly on research areas such as sensing, perception, sensor fusion, data analysis, state estimation, localization and mapping, i.e., Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM), and Artificial Intelligence (AI) applied to various image processing problems, in a compartmentalized manner." The claim's DESCRIPTIVE assertion — that the community treats these as distinct/compartmentalized research areas — is directly and word-for-word supported. Source is primary and legitimate; the claim is mundane, not extraordinary. Independently corroborated by web search (separate literatures/curricula/conference tracks for state estimation, sensor fusion, SLAM, perception) — no contradicting source found. Current (subfield structure stable 2023-2026). CAVEAT worth flagging but NOT refuting: the paper NORMATIVELY argues AGAINST compartmentalization, advocating an integrating "situational awareness" framework; it should not be read as endorsing permanent separation. However, its pro-integration thesis presupposes and confirms the descriptive compartmentalization fact, and for a descriptive taxonomy of engineering competencies the paper's own enumeration of these as distinct "research areas" legitimately validates them as separate Axis-A niches (with integration being a higher-order layer on top, not a dissolution of the underlying competencies). The strongest refutation (that the claim inverts the paper's thesis) fails because it conflates descriptive with normative.
result.counterSource: The same source's normative thesis (advocating integrated "situational awareness" over compartmentalization) is the only counter-current, plus the broader 2024-2026 end-to-end/foundation-model trend — but neither refutes the descriptive claim; both presuppose the compartmentalization they seek to overcome.

result.evidence: Verified verbatim. WebFetch of the source (redirects to pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10222985/) confirms the exact quote in "From SLAM to Situational Awareness: Challenges and Survey," a peer-reviewed survey in Sensors (MDPI), May 2023: "the robotics community has focused chiefly on research areas such as sensing, perception, sensor fusion, data analysis, state estimation, localization and mapping, i.e., Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM), and Artificial Intelligence (AI) applied to various image processing problems, in a compartmentalized manner." The word "compartmentalized" and every listed area match the claim exactly; the claim's ellipsis only drops the "i.e., SLAM, and AI applied to image processing" clause without changing meaning. The descriptive assertion (the community treats these as distinct/compartmentalized areas) is directly supported, and two independent WebSearches corroborate that sensing, perception, sensor fusion, state estimation, and localization/mapping (SLAM) are universally recognized as separate robotics subfields — this is mundane, textbook-level consensus, not an extraordinary claim, so the peer-reviewed survey is more than adequate sourcing. Not outdated (2023, structure unchanged), not marketing. One caveat that does NOT refute: the paper's thesis actually CRITICIZES this compartmentalization and advocates integration via Situational Awareness; however, that normative stance presupposes and confirms the descriptive fact that these ARE currently treated as separate areas, so it does not undermine using the fact to list them as distinct Axis-A niches. The claim is appropriately hedged ("several of these," not all — correctly excluding "data analysis," which is not a robotics-specific niche).
result.counterSource: None found. No credible source disputes that these are distinct robotics research areas; independent searches and the source itself (even while arguing for integration) confirm the compartmentalization. The only nuance is the source's pro-integration thesis, which does not contradict the descriptive claim.

result.summary: The taxonomy of "AI for robotics" resolves cleanly into two orthogonal axes, and the verified evidence strongly supports this separation: Axis A holds technical-capability niches (engineering competencies deployed across domains) and Axis B holds application verticals that layer domain knowledge, regulation, specialized hardware, and a distinct safety envelope on top of the capability core. The 2024–2026 frontier of Axis A is the strongest-evidenced region — robot foundation models / Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, world/predictive models, sim-to-real transfer and simulation/digital twins, and learning-based manipulation/imitation learning are each corroborated by multiple primary 2025–2026 surveys as distinct capability niches built on top of vision+language foundation models, NOT application verticals (the single most common misplacement, alongside tactile/multimodal sensing being a perception capability rather than a vertical). Classical Axis-A niches (perception, multimodal sensor fusion, state estimation, SLAM/localization/mapping, motion planning, model-based vs. learned control, locomotion) are documented as long-standing compartmentalized research areas, and multi-module manipulation frameworks demonstrate the MECE separability of these sub-niches. On Axis B, verticals such as hazardous/explosive-environment robotics carry concrete, non-transferable regulatory and hardware envelopes (ATEX/IECEx protection methods and zone classification), illustrating how a vertical differentiates on top of shared capabilities. A useful umbrella term — "Physical AI" — sits above the entire capability stack, though it is partly a marketing framing and is best treated as a meta-layer rather than a peer niche.

findings.claim: AI for robotics decomposes into two orthogonal axes — Axis A technical-capability niches (competencies deployed across domains) and Axis B application verticals (which add domain knowledge, regulation, specialized hardware, and a distinct safety envelope on top of the capability core). Commonly-misplaced items — VLA/robot foundation models, world models, sim-to-real, and tactile/multimodal sensing — are all Axis-A capabilities, not verticals.
findings.evidence: Multiple primary surveys treat these as horizontal capabilities imported across domains: arXiv 2404.18201 frames robot foundation models as importing computer-vision/NLP FM capabilities INTO manipulation (a capability, not a domain); arXiv 2603.06749 shows the industrial vertical LAYERING cobot/sensing/edge/safety requirements on top of the RFM capability core; PMC10222985 (Sensors 2023) documents sensing, perception, sensor fusion, state estimation, localization and mapping as distinct 'compartmentalized' capability areas. The VLA surveys uniformly classify VLA as a model/policy-learning class, never an industry. Unanimous across all contributing claims.

findings.claim: Robot foundation models / Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are a distinct, FRONTIER (2024–2026) Axis-A capability niche that unifies vision, language, and action at scale to learn generalist policies that generalize across tasks, objects, embodiments, and environments. Sub-niches include FM types (LLMs, Vision Foundation Models, VLMs, VLAs) and architectural paradigms (autoregression-, diffusion-, reinforcement-based, hybrid, specialized; equivalently end-to-end vs. modular vs. compact/efficient). Named exemplars: RT-1/RT-2/RT-X, OpenVLA (open-source 7B), Physical Intelligence π0, NVIDIA GR00T N1, Google Gemini Robotics.
findings.evidence: Nine merged claims converge across at least five independent primary 2025–2026 surveys plus peer-reviewed exemplar papers. Verbatim-confirmed: VLA 'marks a paradigm shift from traditional policy-based control to generalized robotics, reframing VLMs from passive sequence generators into active agents' (arXiv 2509.19012); RFMs 'are emerging as a promising route towards flexible, instruction- and demonstration-driven robot control' (arXiv 2603.06749). The four-way FM-type split (LLM/VFM/VLM/VLA) is taken verbatim from arXiv 2604.15395 (April 2026 review); the five architectural paradigms from arXiv 2509.19012. Emerging flag confirmed by IEEE Access 2025 survey (arXiv 2510.07077) and the April 2026 review. Exemplars verified: OpenVLA 7B outperforming 55B RT-2-X (arXiv 2406.09246, CoRL 2024); GR00T N1 (arXiv 2503.14734).

findings.claim: World models / predictive models — learned representations of how environments evolve under actions — are a distinct, FOUNDATIONAL Axis-A capability niche whose scope spans policy learning, planning, simulation, evaluation, and synthetic-data generation. Commercial 'world foundation models' (e.g., NVIDIA Cosmos) are an active, adopted instance, and synthetic-data generation is a live adjacent niche.
findings.evidence: The comprehensive 2026 survey arXiv 2605.00080 (authored by top robot-learning researchers incl. Abbeel, Malik, Wu, Du, Pollefeys, Torr, Mees, Harada) defines world models as 'a central component of robot learning' supporting 'policy learning, planning, simulation, evaluation, data generation.' NVIDIA Cosmos WFMs (verbatim: 'downloaded over 2 million times, let developers generate diverse data for training robots at scale using text, image and video prompts') evidence real traction with named adopters (1X, Agility, XPENG). The arXiv 2404.18201 manipulation framework independently includes a distinct 'Transition' (dynamics/world-model) module. World models are distinguished from hand-built simulators, avoiding MECE overlap with simulation/digital twins.

findings.claim: Manipulation & dexterous grasping and imitation learning / learning-from-demonstration are FOUNDATIONAL (not merely specialized) Axis-A niches. Learning-based methods are the effective route to generalizable manipulation in unstructured environments, and contact-rich manipulation — a sub-area spanning force/compliant control and tactile/multimodal sensing — is an established, actively-surveyed sub-niche where multimodal learning and foundation models have measurably improved performance.
findings.evidence: arXiv 2404.18201 frames object manipulation in unstructured environments as 'a key hurdle' for general-purpose robots and states 'the learning-based approach is considered an effective way to address generalization.' arXiv 2506.13498 (a dedicated survey published in IJRR 2026, authors incl. Google DeepMind's Nori and IIT's Ajoudani) 'comprehensively surveys research trends in imitation learning for contact-rich robotic tasks' and confirms 'multimodal learning and foundation models have significantly enhanced performance in complex contact tasks across industrial, household, and healthcare domains' — the latter also bridging Axis-A capabilities to three Axis-B verticals.

findings.claim: Locomotion (legged/bipedal/quadruped, and by extension wheeled/aerial/underwater) is a distinct Axis-A capability advanced over ~three decades by two paradigm families — model-based control and learning-based methods — validating both 'locomotion' and the model-based-vs-learned split within 'control' as Axis-A niches.
findings.evidence: Peer-reviewed IJRR 2025 survey (arXiv 2406.01152) by leading legged-robotics researchers states verbatim 'Both model-based and learning-based approaches have advanced the field of legged locomotion in the past three decades,' and that learning-based approaches 'complement the rich literature on model-based control methods.' Single but top-tier authoritative primary source; the model-based-vs-learned control dichotomy is field-standard and reinforced by the foundation-model/RL findings.

findings.claim: Sim-to-real transfer and simulation/digital twins are distinct, tool-backed Axis-A capability niches: the sim-to-real gap systematically degrades RL policies transferred from simulation to real robots, and named open-source frameworks (NVIDIA Isaac Sim 5.0 / Isaac Lab 2.2, alongside MuJoCo, Gazebo, Genesis) exist explicitly to close it.
findings.evidence: arXiv 2009.13303 (IEEE SSCI 2020 survey) establishes that 'the gap between the simulated and real worlds degrades the performance of the policies once the models are transferred into real robots,' motivating a dedicated literature (corroborated by 2024–2025 sim-to-real papers). NVIDIA's press release names Isaac Sim 5.0 / Isaac Lab 2.2 'open-source robot simulation and learning frameworks' that 'help robot developers close the simulation-to-reality gap' and references 'physically accurate digital twins.' NOTE: the proposed five-method sub-structure of sim-to-real (domain randomization, domain adaptation, imitation learning, meta-learning, knowledge distillation) was REFUTED (1-2) and is therefore excluded as an unverified breakdown.
findings.vote: 3-0 across merged claims (5,15); internal sub-niche breakdown refuted 1-2

findings.claim: Perception, multimodal sensor fusion, state estimation, localization, SLAM, and mapping are treated by the robotics community as distinct, compartmentalized research areas — validating each as a separate Axis-A capability niche (with perception sub-niches: computer vision, tactile/force sensing, proprioception, event-based/neuromorphic vision, depth/LiDAR/radar, 3D scene understanding).
findings.evidence: The peer-reviewed survey 'From SLAM to Situational Awareness' (Sensors 2023, PMC10222985) states verbatim that the robotics community 'has focused chiefly on research areas such as sensing, perception, sensor fusion, data analysis, state estimation, localization and mapping ... in a compartmentalized manner.' Cited descriptively (the paper's normative thesis argues for MORE integration, which reinforces that these are currently-distinct niches). Corroborated by the broad literature (EKF/UKF for state estimation, multi-sensor SLAM for mapping). Single primary source but uncontroversial and field-standard.
findings.vote: 3-0 (single primary survey, corroborated by broad literature)

findings.claim: The manipulation stack is decomposable into a multi-module framework (analogous to autonomous driving) whose modules map onto distinct Axis-A sub-niches — perception/state, human-robot interaction, skill hierarchy/planning, policy + action generation (learned control), a dynamics 'Transition'/world-model module, post-condition/success detection, and manipulation data generation — providing direct evidence for the MECE separability of these capability niches rather than a monolith.
findings.evidence: arXiv 2404.18201 proposes an eight-module framework and states 'this framework should encompass multiple functional modules, with different foundation models assuming distinct roles.' Verified against the source (Section 2): the 8 numbered modules are Human/Agent Interaction, Pre-/Post-conditions Detection, Hierarchy of skills, State, Policy, Action, Transition (which 'directly outputs the changed state of the environment and objects' — a dynamics/world model), and Manipulation Data Generation. Single survey's specific schema, but the separability conclusion is reinforced by Findings on FM-types and world models.

findings.claim: Application verticals (Axis B) layer domain-specific requirements on top of the Axis-A capability core: industrial deployment of robot foundation models is shaped by four cross-cutting requirement areas — collaborative robot platforms (HRI/cobots), heterogeneous sensing and actuation (multimodal sensor fusion), edge-computing constraints (embedded/edge AI), and safety-critical operation (safety/verification) — demonstrating how a vertical adds a distinct envelope over shared capabilities.
findings.evidence: arXiv 2603.06749 analyzes 'how industrial domains and use cases shape the requirements of RFMs, with particular focus on collaborative robot platforms, heterogeneous sensing and actuation, edge-computing constraints, and safety-critical operation' (149 criteria, 324 RFMs). These four map cleanly onto four Axis-A niches (cobots/HRI, sensor fusion, edge AI, safety) that the industrial vertical layers on top — the correct Axis-A/Axis-B relationship. Independently corroborated by the sibling 2026 survey arXiv 2603.16952 ('Embodied Foundation Models at the Edge'), which lists the same bottleneck cluster.

findings.claim: Hazardous / explosive-environment robotics (an Axis-B vertical spanning oil & gas, mining, chemical, and nuclear/hazmat) is differentiated by a concrete, non-transferable regulatory and hardware envelope: three core ATEX/IECEx protection methods (intrinsic safety Ex i / energy-limiting; flameproof enclosure Ex d / explosion-containing; pressurization Ex p / atmosphere-excluding) plus zone classification by frequency of explosive atmosphere (gas Zones 0/1/2; dust Zones 20/21/22), where certification is zone-specific and non-transferable upward.
findings.evidence: The RoboticsTomorrow/Armadex article (Nov 2025) names all three protection methods with their exact mechanisms and states 'A robot certified for Zone 2 cannot operate in Zone 1; an entire redesign for Zone 1 must be made.' Secondary vendor source, but corroborated against PRIMARY standards: Ex i = IEC 60079-11, Ex d = IEC 60079-1, Ex p = IEC 60079-2; zone definitions from IEC 60079-10-1/-10-2. This vertical is illustrative of Axis-B differentiators generally — other verticals (defense/ITAR, surgical, agriculture, aviation/UAS, space, marine, autonomous vehicles, food) carry analogous but distinct envelopes that were not independently verified in this pass.
findings.vote: 3-0 (claims 18,19); secondary vendor source corroborated by primary IEC 60079 standards

findings.claim: 'Physical AI' (embodied AI unifying perception, reasoning, and action in the real world) functions as an UMBRELLA / meta-layer above the VLA / world-model / foundation-model robotics stack, and a taxonomy should represent it as a top-level framing rather than a peer capability niche.
findings.evidence: The community list github.com/keon/awesome-physical-ai defines Physical AI as systems 'combining perception, reasoning, and action in real-world environments' and explicitly treats VLA/foundation/world models as 'major implementation categories within it, not competing fields.' Corroborated by NVIDIA Glossary and IBM. WEAKEST finding: primary source is a community 'awesome list' (blog-quality), the only SPLIT vote in the set (2-1), and multiple credible sources (IBM, TechInformed) call Physical AI at least partly a 'marketing buzzword'/rebrand of embodied robotics. Include as an umbrella term with this caveat.

result.caveats: Coverage is deliberately uneven relative to the question's full scope, and this must be stated plainly. The verification pass concentrated on the 2024–2026 Axis-A frontier (foundation models/VLA, world models, sim-to-real, manipulation/imitation learning, locomotion) and on the capability-vs-vertical separation; those conclusions rest on multiple primary 2025–2026 surveys with unanimous 3-0 votes and are high-confidence. However, MANY niches named in the question were NOT independently re-verified and are carried on field-standard scaffolding that the evidence corroborates only indirectly: most classical Axis-A capabilities (proprioception, event-based/neuromorphic vision, depth/LiDAR/radar, 3D scene understanding, MPC/whole-body/compliant/loco-manipulation control, motion planning's classical-vs-sampling-vs-learned split, soft robotics, multi-robot/swarm, formal-methods/runtime-assurance safety, ROS/ROS2/DDS middleware, calibration/systems integration, fleet orchestration and data infrastructure) and nearly ALL Axis-B verticals except hazardous-environments and industrial (defense/ITAR & security clearance, healthcare/surgical, agriculture, construction, mining, oil & gas, autonomous vehicles, aerial/UAS/drones, space, marine/ROV-AUV, inspection & maintenance, food processing, nuclear/decommissioning) — so their completeness and MECE-ness is asserted, not proven, by this pass. Source-quality caveats: the hazardous-environment vertical rests on a secondary vendor article (mitigated by primary IEC 60079 corroboration), and 'Physical AI' rests on a community GitHub list with a 2-1 split vote and explicit 'buzzword' criticism — treat it as the softest item. One sub-structure was actively REFUTED: the proposed five-method breakdown of sim-to-real (domain randomization, domain adaptation, imitation learning, meta-learning, knowledge distillation) did not survive verification (1-2), so sim-to-real's internal sub-niche structure is unsettled. Time-sensitivity: the frontier moves fast — named exemplars (π0, GR00T N1, Gemini Robotics, Cosmos, Isaac Sim 5.0/Lab 2.2 versions) and vendor metrics ('2M+ Cosmos downloads') are point-in-time as of mid-2026 and will date quickly; foundational-vs-emerging labels for VLA and world models may migrate toward 'foundational' within the 2024–2026 window.
openQuestions: What is the authoritative sub-niche decomposition of 'sim-to-real transfer / domain randomization'? The proposed five-method taxonomy (domain randomization, domain adaptation, imitation learning, meta-learning, knowledge distillation) was refuted, leaving the niche confirmed but its internal structure unresolved.

Are the Axis-B application verticals collectively exhaustive and mutually exclusive? Most (defense/ITAR, surgical, agriculture, construction, mining, oil & gas, autonomous vehicles, aerial/UAS, space, marine, nuclear, food) were carried from the question's scaffolding and were not independently verified to each carry a distinct regulatory/safety/hardware envelope warranting a separate niche.

Where do cross-cutting hybrids belong in a strictly MECE tree — e.g., loco-manipulation, whole-body control, humanoids, teleoperation/LfD data pipelines, and fleet/data infrastructure — as Axis-A sub-niches, standalone top-level niches, or capability bundles that legitimately span multiple parents (the main MECE stress point)?

Should umbrella terms like 'Physical AI' and 'embodied AI' be encoded in the taxonomy at all, or kept as an informal meta-layer above both axes, given the unresolved 'genuine field vs. marketing rebrand' debate in the sources?
